lesson.title: Roofing & Carpentry
lesson.explanation: Roof structures: truss types — fink (most common for spans up to 10m), king post, queen post. SA commonly uses SA pine (H3 CCA treated for termites). Roof covering: concrete tiles (heavy, need strong trusses), IBR metal sheeting (lightweight, cheaper), fibre cement. Minimum roof pitch: 15° for tiles, 5° for IBR. Flashing at wall-roof junctions prevents leaks. Gutters and downpipes: PVC or galvanised steel. Ceiling: brandering at 450mm centres, gypsum board or fibre cement. Insulation: minimum R3.7 for SANS 10400-XA energy compliance.

practice_problems.question: A house has a roof span of 8m with a 26° pitch. Calculate the rafter length (from wall plate to ridge), the ridge height above the wall plate, and recommend the appropriate truss type.
practice_problems.answer: Half-span = 8/2 = 4m. Rafter length = half-span / cos(26°) = 4 / 0.8988 = 4.45m. Ridge height = half-span x tan(26°) = 4 x 0.4877 = 1.95m above wall plate. For an 8m span at 26°, a fink (W) truss is the most common and economical choice. It uses internal web members in a W-pattern to transfer roof loads to the wall plates. Fink trusses are factory-manufactured to engineered specifications and can span up to 10m without intermediate support. At 900mm centres (typical for concrete tile roofing), the roof would need: 8m wall length / 0.9m spacing + 1 = approximately 10 trusses per side.
hints: Rafter length uses cos (adjacent/hypotenuse). Ridge height uses tan (opposite/adjacent).
26° is a typical pitch for concrete tile roofing in SA.
practice_problems.explanation: Roof geometry calculations are essential for ordering trusses and estimating material quantities. The pitch determines both the truss design and the covering material options.
distractors: Use the full span for calculations, not half-span
All trusses are the same regardless of span
Rafter length equals the horizontal span

practice_problems.question: Compare concrete roof tiles, IBR metal sheeting, and fibre cement corrugated sheeting for a new house in Johannesburg. Consider weight, cost, minimum pitch, lifespan, and noise.
practice_problems.answer: Concrete tiles: Weight ~50 kg/m² (heaviest, needs stronger trusses). Cost moderate. Minimum pitch 26° (17° with waterproofing membrane). Lifespan 50+ years. Noise: excellent -- mass absorbs sound. Best for: traditional houses where appearance and sound insulation matter. IBR (inverted box rib) metal: Weight ~5 kg/m² (lightest). Cost cheapest. Minimum pitch 5° (3° with sealed joints). Lifespan 25-40 years (depends on coating: chromadek/colorcoat lasts longer). Noise: worst -- very loud in rain and hail (Joburg hailstorms!). Mitigation: insulation + ceiling essential. Best for: budget builds, industrial, flat-pitch roofs. Fibre cement: Weight ~15 kg/m². Cost moderate. Minimum pitch 15°. Lifespan 30-40 years. Noise: moderate. Best for: mid-range builds. Note: older fibre cement contained asbestos (removed pre-2008) -- never cut or disturb old fibre cement without testing. For Johannesburg specifically, the hail risk makes concrete tiles the most durable choice, though IBR with proper insulation is acceptable.
hints: Johannesburg has frequent hailstorms. Metal roofing dents; concrete tiles chip but are replaceable individually.
Roof weight determines truss design and spacing -- heavier coverings need stronger trusses at closer centres.
practice_problems.explanation: Roof covering selection involves balancing cost, performance, aesthetics, and local climate conditions. SA builders must consider regional factors like Joburg hail and coastal corrosion.
distractors: IBR is the best option for all situations because it's cheapest
All roof coverings work at any pitch

practice_problems.question: SANS 10400-XA requires minimum R3.7 thermal resistance for ceilings in Joburg (Climatic Zone 2). A client has 50mm glass wool insulation (R1.3). What additional insulation is needed, and where should it be installed?
practice_problems.answer: Required: R3.7. Existing: R1.3. Additional needed: R3.7 - R1.3 = R2.4. Options: (1) Add 100mm glass wool blanket (R2.5) over the existing insulation in the roof space. Total: R1.3 + R2.5 = R3.8 (meets requirement). (2) Add 50mm rigid polyisocyanurate (PIR) board (R2.5) -- more expensive but thinner. Installation: lay the new insulation OVER the existing layer, perpendicular to the ceiling joists to avoid thermal bridging (heat escaping through the timber). Do not compress the existing insulation (compression reduces R-value). Ensure no gaps at edges, joins, or around downlights. Downlight covers (fire hoods) must be used where insulation contacts recessed lights. Ventilation: maintain eave ventilation to prevent moisture buildup in the roof space. Block insulation from covering ventilation openings.
hints: R-values are additive: layering insulation adds R-values together.
Thermal bridging occurs where timber (poor insulator) penetrates the insulation layer.
practice_problems.explanation: SANS 10400-XA compliance is mandatory for all new builds and major renovations. Ceiling insulation is the most cost-effective energy saving measure in SA homes.
distractors: Compressing insulation makes it more effective
Insulation is only needed in cold climates

practice_problems.question: During a roof inspection, you find that the timber trusses are SA pine but show no CCA treatment stamps, and several have green-brown fungal growth. What are the risks and required actions?
practice_problems.answer: Risks: (1) Untreated SA pine is susceptible to termite attack (subterranean termites are common in SA) and wood-boring beetles. (2) Fungal growth indicates moisture exposure. Fungal decay (wet rot, dry rot) weakens timber by breaking down cellulose fibres, reducing load-bearing capacity. A truss that has lost structural integrity could collapse under roof load. Actions: (1) Test timber strength: probe affected areas with a screwdriver. Soft, crumbly wood = significant decay. (2) Measure moisture content with a pin moisture meter: >20% = decay risk, >28% = active decay likely. (3) Identify the moisture source: roof leak, inadequate ventilation, blocked gutter overflowing into roof space. Fix the source FIRST. (4) Replace severely affected trusses (cannot be repaired). (5) Treat all timber with borate-based preservative (brush-applied, penetrates existing timber). (6) Ensure roof space ventilation (eave vents, ridge vent) to keep moisture below 18%. SANS 10400-H requires H3 CCA-treated timber for roof structures in termite-risk areas (most of SA).
hints: CCA treatment leaves a green tint on timber. No green tint + no stamp = likely untreated.
Fix the moisture source before treating the timber -- otherwise treatment is wasted.
practice_problems.explanation: Roof timber condition directly affects structural safety. Untreated timber in SA's climate is a serious risk. SANS 10400 requires treated timber for good reason.
distractors: Untreated timber is fine if the roof doesn't leak
Painting the timber is equivalent to CCA treatment
Fungal growth is cosmetic and doesn't affect strength

practice_problems.question: A valley (internal angle where two roof planes meet) is leaking during heavy rain. Describe the correct valley flashing installation per good practice, and identify three common causes of valley leaks.
practice_problems.answer: Correct installation: (1) Valley board (treated timber) supports the flashing between the rafters/trusses. (2) Valley flashing: galvanised steel or aluminium sheet, minimum 450mm wide (225mm each side of the valley centre line). For concrete tiles, use a purpose-made valley trough. (3) Tiles/sheets should overhang the flashing by at least 75mm but not more than 150mm. (4) Flashing overlaps at joints: minimum 150mm, sealed with appropriate sealant, lapped in the direction of water flow (upper sheet overlaps lower). (5) Valley extends into the gutter at the bottom. Three common leak causes: (1) Insufficient flashing width: heavy rain overflows the edge of narrow flashing, especially where two large roof planes channel water into the valley. (2) Debris accumulation: leaves and dirt dam up in the valley, forcing water sideways under tiles. Solution: regular cleaning. (3) Tiles cut too close to valley centre: reduces the overlap, allowing wind-driven rain to enter. Always cut tiles to leave 75mm minimum overhang on each side.
hints: Valleys carry the highest concentration of water on a roof -- they are the most common leak point.
Wind-driven rain travels sideways, not just downward -- flashings must account for this.
practice_problems.explanation: Valley waterproofing requires attention to detail. Most roof leaks occur at junctions (valleys, wall-roof intersections, chimneys) rather than in the middle of roof planes.
distractors: Valley flashing is optional if tiles fit tightly
Any width of flashing is acceptable
Valleys never need maintenance once installed

misconceptions.misconception: You don't need formal training for Roofing & Carpentry
misconceptions.correction: Proper training and certification are required for safety and legal compliance in SA.
misconceptions.why_students_think_this: People see informal work and assume no qualifications are needed.
